summary: Drew DeVault calls for a protest against Jack Dorsey's keynote at FOSDEM 2025, arguing that billionaires should not be platformed at free software events. He details Dorsey's role in selling Twitter to Elon Musk, the resulting harm to the FOSS community which absorbed fleeing users, and Block's sponsorship as the likely reason for the keynote slot. DeVault connects Dorsey's company to layoffs, bitcoin mining powered by fossil fuels, and broader tech grift. He announces a planned sit-in to peacefully block the talk and argues billionaires should earn FOSS community respect through massive donations rather than keynote stages.
key_points: The FOSDEM Janson keynote stage is one of the biggest platforms in free software and should reflect community values, not sponsor influence
Dorsey sold Twitter to Musk for a billion dollars, triggering a fediverse exodus that FOSS volunteers subsidized with their labor and money
Block's sponsorship of FOSDEM is likely buying Dorsey the keynote slot to promote blockchain and bitcoin mining ventures
Dorsey's bitcoin mining operations are linked to fossil fuel power plants causing CO2 emissions and estimated deaths from pollution
DeVault proposes billionaires earn FOSS community respect through proportional donations rather than using community platforms for self-promotion
money_quotes: In my view, billionaires are not welcome at FOSDEM.
I don't think that we owe this honor to someone who took a billion dollars to ruin their project, ostracize their users, and destroy the livelihoods of almost everyone who worked on it.

After Dorsey sold Twitter to Elon Musk, selling the platform out to the far right for a crisp billion-with-a-"B" dollar payout, the FOSS community shouldered the burden – both with our labor and our wallets – of a massive exodus onto our volunteer-operated servers.

If billionaires want to participate in FOSS, I'm going to ask them to refrain from using our platforms to talk about their AI/blockchain/bitcoin/climate-disaster-as-a-service grifty business ventures, and instead buy our respect by, say, donating 250 million dollars to NLNet or the Sovereign Tech Fund.
That figure, as a percentage of Dorsey's wealth, is proportional to the amount of money I donate to FOSS every year, by the way.

I am organizing a sit-in, in which I and anyone who will join me are going to sit ourselves down on the Janson stage during his allocated time slot and peacefully prevent the talk from proceeding as scheduled.
key_insight: Free software community platforms should not be sold to billionaires whose wealth was built by harming the very communities those platforms serve.
